evidence: Runtime callback bags are deeply nested and split by concern layers: `startupInitCoreRuntime`, `startupInitHostRuntime`, `startupInitStorageRuntime`, and `StartupRuntime` in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`, plus similarly layered `importerStorageRuntime`/`importerUploadRuntime`/`importerProvisionRuntime`/`importerRuntime` in `goseg/importer/importer.go`.

Multiple methods are indirection-only wrappers that mainly nil-check and forward (`bootstrap`, `startServer`, `runC2CCheck` in `goseg/main.go`; `StartConfEventLoop` and `StartConfEventLoopWithRuntime` aliases in `goseg/config/config_events.go`; `LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntime` forwarding in `goseg/broadcast/state_lifecycle.go`).

Domain workflows embed broad infrastructure runtime structs (`dockerOrchestration.RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, `RuntimeSnapshotOps`) in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` and `goseg/importer/importer.go`, which increases surface area each caller must reason about.

Within this batch, dependencies are mostly centralized and consistent around Docker SDK usage (`github.com/docker/docker/...`) in orchestration/network/events files rather than duplicated third-party stacks for the same purpose.

No direct import-cycle evidence was found in the assigned files; dependency use is largely package-internal plus standard library and a small set of stable external libs (`zap`, Docker SDK, websocket, wifi client).
Event publishing is abstracted via `EventBroker` in `goseg/docker/events/events.go`, which allows delegation without introducing extra dependency variants.
dependency_health.issues_preventing_higher_score: Runtime structs in workflow/importer still embed broad orchestration dependency surfaces, so dependency boundaries are functional but not yet minimal or strongly constrained.

evidence: holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles shows three overloaded flat directories relevant to this batch: goseg/config/ (40 files), goseg/shipworkflow/ (44 files), and goseg/system/ (39 files), each above the >10 mixed-concern threshold.
holistic_context flags already identify flat_dirs findings for these directories: goseg/config, goseg/shipworkflow, and goseg/system.

In assigned files, goseg/system/disk_fstab.go and goseg/system/disk_health.go are root-level system package wrappers that primarily delegate to groundseg/system/storage (import-affinity concentrated on storage concerns), indicating boundary leakage between orchestration-facing APIs and storage implementation placement.

Assigned shipworkflow files include minimal lifecycle core plus placeholder test (lifecycle_test.go), while the directory holds many domains (newship, startram, urbit operations, workflow helpers), suggesting broad mixed ownership in one folder.

Configuration ownership is split between process-runtime context and unrelated Startram cache state in one module (`goseg/config/config.go`), while orchestration wiring pulls that global state directly (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`).

Action API surface in `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` repeats namespace-specific wrappers (`ParseUploadAction`/`ParseC2CAction`, `SupportedUploadActions`/`SupportedC2CActions`, metadata/compatibility pairs), indicating branch-per-namespace scaling instead of data-driven dispatch.

Contract conformance checks are repeated in separate test modules (`goseg/uploadsvc/service_test.go`, `goseg/system/wifi_c2c_flow_test.go`) with each scanning fixtures independently rather than sharing one domain-level contract assertion path.

Large tests repeatedly perform manual global-state restore patterns (`goseg/main_test.go`, `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator_test.go`, `goseg/handler/ship/urbit_test.go`, `goseg/importer/importer_test.go`), showing hidden coupling to mutable singletons.

Config package mixes two competing vocabularies for the same concept: `StartConfigEventLoop`/`StartConfigEventLoopWithRuntime` alongside alias and internals `StartConfEventLoop`, `runConfEventLoop`, `processConfEvent`, and `Conf()` in goseg/config/config_events.go.

WiFi API/methods use three names for the same interface identity (`dev`, `device`, `ifaceName`) and include opaque names like `ifCheck`, making call sites less self-explanatory across goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go and goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go.

Multiple orchestration modules overload `runtime` to mean different things (dependency bundle, lifecycle state, service group, context object), e.g. `StartupRuntime`, `startupInitRuntime`, `startBackgroundServicesRuntime`, `workflowRuntime`, `RuntimeContainerOps`, reducing semantic precision across goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go, goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go, and goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go.

findings.summary: Layered runtime callback bags create high indirection without a clear domain boundary contract.
evidence: `startuporchestrator` defines multiple nested runtime structs and then recomposes them (`startupInitCoreRuntime`, `startupInitHostRuntime`, `startupInitStorageRuntime`, `startupInitRuntime`, `StartupRuntime`) before execution.
`importer` repeats the same pattern with split runtime structs and conversion helpers (`initRuntime`, `workflowRuntime`, `publishRuntime`) that mostly reshape callback bags.
`workflowRuntime` embeds large orchestration ops groups plus local callbacks, forcing consumers to carry a wide mixed-purpose runtime regardless of specific operation needs.
findings.suggestion: Introduce task-scoped capability interfaces (for example: startup bootstrap port, import provisioning port, transition emitter port) and have runtime constructors assemble only those minimal interfaces per use-case; remove intermediate bag-to-bag conversion helpers where no policy translation occurs.

findings.summary: Several `WithRuntime`/alias wrappers add call depth but little behavior, making control flow harder to follow.
evidence: `LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntime` and related broadcast helpers primarily validate runtime presence and forward to another function with near-identical semantics.
`StartConfigEventLoop`, `StartConfEventLoop`, `StartConfigEventLoopWithRuntime`, and `StartConfEventLoopWithRuntime` form backward-compatible aliases that fragment the canonical path.
Routine wrappers like `StartBackupRoutines` / `StartBackupRoutinesWithContext` and `StartChopRoutines` / `StartChopRoutinesWithContext` duplicate lifecycle entrypoints with thin forwarding behavior.
findings.suggestion: Define one canonical execution entrypoint per concern, keep compatibility aliases as deprecated shims in a dedicated compatibility file, and collapse no-op forwarding layers into direct calls unless they enforce policy/translation.

findings.summary: Business workflows depend on broad infrastructure operation sets instead of narrow use-case ports.
evidence: `importerProvisionRuntime` directly embeds orchestration runtime operation groups and many concrete callbacks, mixing import workflow concerns with container/volume mechanics.
`workflowRuntime` similarly embeds container/urbit/snapshot ops rather than a reduced transition-specific contract.
`runtime_ops.go` exposes large horizontal operation structs that are reused wholesale, encouraging consumers to take more dependency surface than needed.
findings.suggestion: Refactor orchestration ops into vertically sliced capability ports per workflow (for example `ShipTransitionOps`, `ImportVolumeOps`, `StartramSyncOps`) and inject only those into importer/workflow services.

findings.summary: Three top-level domain folders exceed flat-directory limits and mix unrelated concerns.
evidence: Directory profiles in holistic_context report goseg/config/ with 40 files, goseg/shipworkflow/ with 44 files, and goseg/system/ with 39 files.
The same packet includes explicit flat_dirs findings for each of these directories.

Within reviewed files, responsibilities already span runtime bootstrapping (config/bootstrap.go), runtime state API (config/config.go), async event loop (config/config_events.go), lifecycle orchestration (shipworkflow/lifecycle.go), and disk/storage delegation (system/disk_*), confirming mixed change boundaries in flat roots.
findings.suggestion: Run a staged reorg. Stage 1: create target folders `goseg/config/runtime`, `goseg/config/events`, `goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle`, `goseg/system/disk`, and keep current packages while moving files incrementally. Stage 2 move order: (1) move `config_events*.go` into `config/events`; (2) move `bootstrap.go` and runtime-context setters from `config.go` into `config/runtime`; (3) move `lifecycle*.go` into `shipworkflow/lifecycle`; (4) move `disk_fstab.go` and `disk_health.go` into `system/disk` with explicit API surface. Stage 3: update imports via `go test ./...` driven compile fixes, then run `gofmt -w goseg/config goseg/shipworkflow goseg/system` and validate with `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Disk adapter files in system root are storage-focused and better placed near storage boundary.
evidence: `disk_fstab.go` and `disk_health.go` mostly forward calls and record mapping to `groundseg/system/storage` functions.
These files expose root `system` package signatures but implementation concern is storage-boundary translation, not general system orchestration.
Placement at `goseg/system/` increases boundary ambiguity because storage-specific seams live outside `goseg/system/storage/`.
findings.suggestion: Use a two-step boundary extraction. Step 1: create `goseg/system/disk/adapter.go` and move fstab/smart/storage forwarding there while keeping compatibility shims in `goseg/system/` during transition. Step 2: switch internal callers to `groundseg/system/disk` imports, then remove shims once compile graph is clean. Validation commands: `go test ./goseg/system/... ./goseg/system/storage/...` then full `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Config runtime state and async event handling are colocated in one root package boundary.
evidence: `bootstrap.go` handles initialization and filesystem path setup, `config.go` owns runtime context snapshots/state APIs, and `config_events.go` runs channel-driven event loops tied to system package events.
These are separate ownership axes (bootstrap/runtime state/event processing) but all remain at one package root in an already 40-file flat directory.
Event loop implementation imports `groundseg/system` directly for channel sourcing, increasing cross-boundary coupling from a broad root package.
findings.suggestion: Apply a staged split under config. Stage 1: move `config_events.go` to `goseg/config/events` and expose `events.StartConfigEventLoop...` API. Stage 2: move runtime-context functions from `config.go` and path/bootstrap code from `bootstrap.go` into `goseg/config/runtime`. Stage 3: keep a thin compatibility facade in `config` that re-exports stable entrypoints, then update call sites with `go test ./goseg/config/...` and finally `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: WiFi C2C ownership is split between flat `system` files and nested `system/wifi` packages.
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` declares C2C governance owner as `system/wifi` for `C2CConnectAction`.
`wifi_c2c_flow_test.go` remains in `package system` and validates C2C flow behavior via root-level seams (`newC2CModeFlowWithDependencies`, `c2cServiceDeps`).
README points to `goseg/system/wifi/README.md` as policy documentation, but runtime/test placement still straddles root `system` and nested `system/wifi`.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/system/wifi/flow/` and move C2C flow runtime + tests from root `goseg/system/wifi_*` files that belong to C2C flow into that subtree; (2) leave thin compatibility shims in `goseg/system/` only for external callers, then remove them after callsites migrate; (3) keep protocol owner metadata aligned with final package path (`system/wifi/...`) and update README governance map to exact target folders. Validation/import update commands: `cd goseg && gofmt -w system/wifi/**/*.go system/*.go && go test ./system/... ./protocol/... && go test -tags=integration ./system/...`.

findings.summary: Backend quality-gate scope in README does not map to contract-owning package boundaries.
evidence: README backend gate runs `go test ./...` plus integration subset `./broadcast ./handler ./routines ./ws`.
Governance-critical contract definitions are in `goseg/protocol/contracts/*` with compatibility metadata and ownership fields.
C2C contract behavior is validated by wifi flow tests under `goseg/system/`, but those ownership boundaries are not reflected in the explicit integration subset.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan for quality-gate/package alignment: (1) define package-boundary test tiers in README by ownership folder (`protocol/contracts`, `system/wifi`, `startram`, `uploadsvc`); (2) add a second explicit command block that targets those folders before broad integration suites; (3) keep move-order docs synchronized with folder moves from the wifi boundary refactor. Validation commands: `cd goseg && go test ./protocol/contracts ./system/wifi/... ./startram/... ./uploadsvc/...` then existing integration command.

findings.summary: Protocol action identity is split across separate packages, increasing cross-file change churn.
evidence: `actionids.go` defines namespace/action literals, while `protocol_contracts.go` rebinds them into typed aliases and contract IDs.
A single action change (e.g., C2C connect) requires coordinated edits across action token constants, contract governance entries, and downstream flow tests (`wifi_c2c_flow_test.go`).
This split is manageable at small scale but creates package-level edit scatter for contract-governed actions.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) introduce `goseg/protocol/contracts/actionspec/` (or `goseg/protocol/contracts/actiondefs/`) as the single source for action namespace+verb+contract metadata tuples; (2) generate/derive `actionids` constants and contract catalog entries from that source in move order: define tuples first, adapt `protocol_contracts.go`, then make `actionids.go` thin re-export or remove it; (3) update imports and run validations. Commands: `cd goseg && go test ./protocol/actions ./protocol/contracts ./system/...` and `rg "NamespaceC2C|C2CConnect|UploadOpenEndpoint" goseg/protocol goseg/system` to verify callsite updates.

findings.summary: Config module mixes unrelated responsibilities and forces cross-subsystem mutable singleton coupling.
evidence: `goseg/config/config.go` contains both runtime process context mutation (`SetBasePath`, `SetArchitecture`, `SetDebugMode`, `SetDockerDir`) and separate Startram cache mutation (`SetStartramConfig`/`GetStartramConfig`) in the same package-level singleton state surface.
`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` wires runtime seams directly to global config getters, coupling orchestration behavior to mutable package state rather than explicit dependency objects.

Multiple tests in different modules must snapshot and restore global config (`config.SetDebugMode` in `goseg/main_test.go`; repeated `config.SetBasePath` save/restore in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator_test.go`; `config.SetStartramConfig` restore in `goseg/handler/ship/urbit_test.go`).
findings.suggestion: Split config into focused services (process runtime context vs Startram snapshot store), pass immutable snapshots/dependencies into orchestration constructors, and centralize test fixtures around scoped runtime providers instead of direct global mutation.

findings.summary: Action contract API repeats namespace-specific wrappers, creating change amplification for each new namespace.
evidence: `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` duplicates near-identical Upload/C2C wrappers for parse/supported-actions/metadata/compatibility helpers, each delegating to generic namespace methods with fixed constants.

Tests mirror this split surface and duplicate validation paths; e.g., upload and C2C behavior is asserted in separate modules (`goseg/uploadsvc/service_test.go`, `goseg/system/wifi_c2c_flow_test.go`) instead of exercising one normalized namespace contract table.

`goseg/protocol/actions/actions_test.go` contains overlapping unknown-upload parse tests (`TestParseUploadActionReportsDeterministicErrorType` and `TestParseUploadActionRejectsUnknown`) signaling API/test sprawl.
findings.suggestion: Collapse namespace-specific wrappers into a table-driven namespace descriptor API (single parse/support/metadata entrypoints + generated convenience aliases only where required) and consolidate tests around shared namespace conformance cases.

findings.summary: Contract conformance checks are fragmented across feature tests instead of centralized, causing duplicated responsibility.
evidence: `goseg/uploadsvc/service_test.go` scans all fixtures and validates upload namespace contracts inline (`TestUploadActionFixturesRemainConformant`).
`goseg/system/wifi_c2c_flow_test.go` performs another fixture scan for C2C alignment (`TestC2CPayloadAndContractsStayAligned`) using a similar loop pattern and failure handling.
`goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts_test.go` already validates registry/binding correctness globally, so additional per-feature fixture scans duplicate structural contract governance logic.
findings.suggestion: Move fixture enumeration/conformance assertions into a single protocol contract test helper package and let feature tests assert only feature behavior against typed actions, not catalog scanning.

findings.summary: Large tests reimplement ad-hoc state/reset harnesses, reducing coherence of test architecture.
evidence: `goseg/importer/importer_test.go` defines multiple local reset/setup helpers (`resetUploadSessionsForTest`, `setUploadDirsForTest`, runtime accessor guards) to manually control global/static state.
`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator_test.go` embeds a large callback-setter map and custom runtime options to wire tests, then still uses repeated global config restoration patterns.
`goseg/main_test.go` and `goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go` use separate per-file seam/reset idioms instead of a shared test runtime fixture abstraction.
findings.suggestion: Introduce shared test runtime fixture builders for global config mutation, seam registration, and callback wiring; migrate repeated per-file setup/reset helpers to this common harness to reduce structural duplication.

findings.summary: Config module uses conflicting `config` vs `conf` naming for the same API surface, obscuring intent and discoverability.
evidence: `StartConfigEventLoop` is paired with aliases/internal names `StartConfEventLoop`, `StartConfEventLoopWithRuntime`, `runConfEventLoop`, `processConfEvent` in the same file.
`processConfEvent` calls `Conf()` while neighboring APIs are `*SettingsSnapshot` and `RuntimeContextSnapshot`, creating mixed terminology for reading config state.
Patch schema/registry types are centered on `confPatch*` prefixes while the package/file naming is `config*`, continuing the split vocabulary.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on a single public and internal term (`config`) and deprecate/remove `conf` identifiers in one migration pass (aliases retained temporarily with clear deprecation comments), including patch-type prefixes.

findings.summary: The label `runtime` is overloaded for unrelated concepts, making orchestration APIs harder to reason about.
evidence: `startup_orchestrator.go` defines `StartupRuntime`, `startupInitRuntime`, and `startBackgroundServicesRuntime` where each represents a different concern (bootstrap callbacks vs background service wiring).
`operations_runtime.go` uses `workflowRuntime` as both dependency carrier and operation context, while `runtime_ops.go` uses many `Runtime*Ops` types for function bundles.
`wifi_runtime_api.go` and `logs.go` also expose service-level runtime structs (`WiFiRuntimeService.runtime`, `LogstreamRuntime`) that are semantically different from orchestration runtime bundles.
findings.suggestion: Rename by role rather than umbrella term (for example `Deps`, `ServiceSet`, `BootstrapHooks`, `WorkflowDeps`) and reserve `runtime` for one narrowly defined concept per subsystem.

findings.summary: WiFi interface identifiers are named inconsistently (`dev`, `device`, `ifaceName`) and include shorthand names that hide behavior.
evidence: API methods mix `RefreshWiFiInfo(dev string)`, `ListWiFiSSIDs(dev string)`, `DisconnectWiFi(ifaceName string)`, and internal `resolveDevice(dev, ...)` for the same conceptual value.
Runtime method `ifCheck()` is non-descriptive compared with surrounding method names (`primaryWifiDevice`, `listSSIDs`, `toggleDevice`).
Mixed call-site wording increases ambiguity around whether parameters represent device name, interface name, or selected state.
findings.suggestion: Adopt one canonical term (`interfaceName` or `deviceName`) across API/runtime/test surfaces and rename opaque helpers like `ifCheck` to behavior-explicit forms (`isWiFiRadioEnabled`/`readWiFiRadioState`).

findings.summary: High reuse of `run*` prefixes across unrelated behaviors reduces semantic signal in execution flows.
evidence: Core entrypoints and helpers use broad verbs (`run`, `runServer`, `runBootstrapSubsystems`, `runStartupSubsystems`, `runRoutine`, `runTransitionLifecycle`, `runImportPhases`) despite different side effects and lifecycles.

In startup and workflow orchestration, several adjacent `run*` functions differ primarily by suffix (`runTransitionLifecycle`, `runTransitionLifecycleResolved`, `runUrbitTransition`), making intent distinctions easy to miss.
This pattern appears in multiple subsystems, indicating naming convention drift rather than isolated local style.
findings.suggestion: Use intent-specific verbs by responsibility (`start`, `execute`, `dispatch`, `publish`, `bootstrap`, `orchestrate`) and keep `run*` for thin loop/executor primitives only.
